elements.sent-2411-001: Large Multi-Modal Models (LMMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities as general-purpose chatbots that can engage in conversations about a provided input, such as an image.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, their responses are influenced by societal biases present in their training datasets, leading to undesirable differences in how the model responds when presented with images depicting people of different demographics.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we propose a novel debiasing framework for LMMs that directly removes biased representations during text generation to decrease outputs related to protected attributes, or even representing them internally.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our proposed method is training-free; given a single image and a list of target attributes, we can ablate the corresponding representations with just one step of gradient descent on the image itself.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our experiments show that not only can we can minimize the propensity of LMMs to generate text related to protected attributes, but we can improve sentiment and even simply use synthetic data to inform the ablation while retaining language modeling capabilities on real data such as COCO or FACET.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we find the resulting generations from a debiased LMM exhibit similar accuracy as a baseline biased model, showing that debiasing effects can be achieved without sacrificing model performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: Deep neural networks are well known to exhibit a myriad of societal biases learned from their training datasets \citep{bolukbasi2016man, zhao2017men .}
elements.sent-2411-008: While this effect is true to some extent for most models, it is especially prevalent in models trained on web-scale data.
elements.sent-2411-009: Numerous prior works have observed and identified such biases present in modern Large Language Models (LLMs) \citep{bender2021dangers, bommasani2021opportunities , while other recent work shows that such biases are even more prevalent in Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) \footnote{LMMs refer specifcally to generative multimodal models.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: We refer to the general class of vision-language models as VLMs \citep{birhane2021multimodal such as LLaVA-1.5 \citep{liu2024visual , or Llama-3.2 \citep{dubey2024llama that integrate a vision backbone with a pretrained LLM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Given that LLMs are often pretrained on relatively uncurated web-scale data \citep{schuhmann2022laion , any further integration with additional models will inherit the particular biases of the LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Without additional safety tuning, these pre-existing biases may be amplified further when an LLM is augmented with pretrained visual capabilities, which also come with a distinct set of implicit societal biases present in the visual pretraining data.
elements.sent-2411-013: Evaluating and mitigating potentially harmful behaviors induced by these societal biases is becoming increasingly important in order to safely deploy multimodal generative AI systems that utilize LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this work we focus on reducing the propensity of an LMM to attend to protected attributes unnecessarily.
elements.sent-2411-015: While it is often benign and even useful to make use of protected attributes in generation, LMMs have been found to suffer from group-dependent sentiment \citep{raj2024biasdora , well as conditional inaccuracies given certain protected attributes \citep{howard2024socialcounterfactuals, zhao2021captionbias .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{figs/figure1_vlm.pdf} \caption{An overview of our model steering approach for LMMs. By solving for an appropriate steering vector we can augment the generation patterns of the LMM by ablating the internal representation corresponding to a given attribute at test time. The steering vector is computed with a single step of gradient optimization to maximize the likelihood of protected tokens. The attribute that we ablate is then computed by taking a difference between original activations and optimized activations.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, a variety of methods have been proposed for debiasing LLMs and Discriminative Vision Language Models individually \citep{lin2024towards,slyman2024fairdedup .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, relatively little prior work has focused specifically on debiasing LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-018: One naive approach could be to simply define a list of words or symbols that are blocked at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-019: While a block-list could have merit in some applications, a more nuanced approach is required for attributes with many or ambiguous meanings, such as perceived race (e.g., black, white)\footnote{Wherever we use words such as race or gender throughout this work, we mean perceived race and perceived gender.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The labels we used for such social attributes are inherited from prior work and are not a reflection of our own value judgments.
elements.sent-2411-022: Many other existing debiasing approaches for LLMs and VLMs focus on fine-tuning models with additional curated data to reduce bias.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, training is a blunt tool that often results in undesirable outcomes such as a degradation in task-specific performance.
elements.sent-2411-024: Fine-tuning is also labor and computationally intensive, requiring the collection of an additional (likely large-scale) dataset that can appropriately debias the model.
elements.sent-2411-025: Despite prior efforts \citep{howard2024uncovering , there remains no cannonical recipe for constructing a dataset large enough to meet data requirements for training LMMs, while also fine-grained enough to ablate specific target attributes like perceived race.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, our work introduces a training-free approach to debiasing LMMs that can be applied to any attribute at inference time without degrading model performance.
elements.sent-2411-027: Inspired by mechanistic interpretability and prior work in steering language models, we propose a novel method for identifying and ablating biased representations present in LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-028: Recently, prior work has shown that it is possible to ablate representations with respect to target attributes by using a small number of contrastive prompts \citep{marks2023geometry in order to identify directions in representation space that correspond to an increased propensity to generate related outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our method differs from prior LLM steering work in two fundamental ways.
elements.sent-2411-030: First, our method targets multimodal models rather than just language models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, our approach requires no additional data other than an input image and a target attribute to remove.
elements.sent-2411-032: We find that we can leverage the additional information available to a LMM, the input image, in order to solve for the steering direction on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-033: Specifically, during inference we perform a single step of gradient descent on the visual representation in the direction of the target attributes.
elements.sent-2411-034: This one-step optimization is similar to the fast sign gradient method (FGSM) \citep{goodfellow2014explaining applied on LMM representations.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We then can perform model steering as normal, by extracting downstream activations that follow from both the perturbed and the unchanged visual representations.
elements.sent-2411-036: The normalized difference between them is used as the steering direction.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose an efficient training-free algorithm to find effective steering directions for LMMs that performs a single optimization step on the input visual representation.
elements.sent-2411-039: We show that our proposed approach can effectively debias two popular LMM model families LLaVA and Llama3.
elements.sent-2411-040: We reduce their propensity to generate text related to protected attributes on the SocialCounterfactuals, COCO, and FACET datasets.
elements.sent-2411-041: We find that our results hold across perceived race, physical appearance, age, and gender attributes.
elements.sent-2411-042: We analyze the steered and unsteered generations across all datasets and find that steering does not degrade accuracy or language modeling capabilities, finding instead, steering produces equitable group-level sentiment.
elements.sent-2411-044: Due to the expense and difficulty of fine-tuning LLMs for arbitrary domains, its natural to seek an efficient and fine-grained approach for controlling LLM outputs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Prior work has attempted to steer generations by intervening on inputs, outputs, or intermedate representations.
elements.sent-2411-046: Modifying prompts to induce or ablate a feature \citep{zhang2024causal,yang2023adept is one such approach, but is commonly an iterative and inefficient method.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Intervening on outputs has been explored via token-level block lists \citep{gehman2020realtoxicityprompts , domain classifiers \citep{lees2022new to trigger regeneration, or decoding strategies that decrease probabilities of generating certain tokens \citep{schick2021self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Intervening on intermediate representations is most relevant to our approach.
elements.sent-2411-049: This often entails the discovery of interpretable features that not only explain model behavior \citep{zou2023representation, huben2024sparse , but can also be used to intervene and steer the model towards output generations with certain characteristics or content.}
elements.sent-2411-050: There, the authors demonstrated the existence of monosemantic features that can trigger relevant downstream behavior or content when manually introduced during inference.
elements.sent-2411-051: Various methods of steering have also been applied to toxiciy \cite{liu2024incontext and other behaviors such as hallucination \cite{rimsky2023steering .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: While several approaches have been proposed for mitigating social biases in VLMs \citep{wang2021gender,berg2022prompt,zhang2022contrastive,seth2023dear,chuang2023debiasing,smith2023balancing,howard2024socialcounterfactuals , prior research on addressing such biases in LMMs is lacking.}
elements.sent-2411-053: They also investigated the usefulness of prompting strategies to reduce bias at inference time, but found that it produced inconsistent debiasing effects across different models and generation settings.
elements.sent-2411-054: While representaiton-based steering for reducing societal biases has been demonstrated in LLMs such as Claude 3 \citep{templeton2024scaling , our work is the first to demonstrate successful inference-time representation steering for reducing bias in LMMs.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.999\textwidth]{figs/architecture_diagram.pdf} \caption{A detailed description depicting our single step gradient-based steering direction computation. (Step 1) Compute the intermediate activations of the LLM when asking the model a direct question about the target attribute to remove. (Step 2) Run a single step of gradient optimization with a logit maximizing loss function in order to compute a modified version of the image tokens $\mathbf{u}$ which increases the probability the model will reference the protected attribute. (Step 3) Recompute the activations of the LLM with the new modified image tokens $\mathbf{u'}$ and take a difference between the two intermediate LLM activations. (Step 4) Run the intended query for the LMM, but ablate the attribute $a$ from all intermediate layers of the LLM.} \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-055: Our approach to debiasing LMMs involves identifying and ablating the bias attribute in the model's internal representations.
elements.sent-2411-056: We achieve this by contrasting the model's original activations from alterated activations: either from a single step gradient optimized version or from a collection of bias invoking questions.
elements.sent-2411-057: An overview of the single step gradient-based method is shown in figure \ref{fig:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Let $\mathcal{M}$ denote an arbitrary LMM, and $\mathbf{h ^{l \in \mathbb{R ^{d}$ represent the activations at layer $l$, where $d$ is the dimensionality of the hidden state.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We use $\mathbf{a \in \mathbb{R ^{d}$ to denote the bias attribute, which is a vector that captures the direction of the bias in the model's internal representations, and define $\mathbf{r ^{l}$ as the residual at layer $l$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: To estimate the bias attribute, we collect a dataset of standard prompt-image pairs $\mathcal{D _{\text{standard = \{(\mathbf{x _i) = (\mathbf{p _i, \mathbf{i _i)\}_{i=1 ^{N_{\text{standard }$ and a dataset of prompt-image pairs which elicit biased responses $\mathcal{D _{\text{bias = \{(\mathbf{x _i) = (\mathbf{p _i, \mathbf{i _i)\}_{i=1 ^{N_{\text{bias }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Here, $\mathbf{p _i$ represents the text prompt and $\mathbf{i _i$ represents the corresponding image.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: We compute the activations of the model on both datasets and calculate the difference in means: \begin{equation}a = \frac{1}{|D_{bias}|} \sum_{x \in D_{bias}} h^{l}(x) - \frac{1}{|D_{standard}|} \sum_{x \in D_{standard}} h^{l}(x)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: By having access to the models weights and a list of words related to a target attribute, we can perform a single step of gradient optimization to create a contrasting set of inputs to the language model.
elements.sent-2411-064: We define $T$ be a list of tokens related to a target attribute.
elements.sent-2411-065: We overload $h^{l (x,u)$ to represent the output of layer $l$ given the input text $x$ and encoded image tokens $u$.}

elements.sent-2411-069: Then we can simply compute the attribute vector with the modified image representation to compute the linear direction corresponding to the attribute: $\mathbf{a = \mathbf{h ^{l (\mathbf{x,u' ) - \mathbf{h ^{l (\mathbf{x,u )$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-071: To ablate the bias attribute, we project the residual at each layer onto the bias attribute and subtract the projection from the residual to get a new residual $\mathbf{r ^{l' = \mathbf{r ^{l - \mathbf{a \mathbf{a ^\top \mathbf{r ^{l}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: We apply this ablation process to every residual in the LMM, effectively removing the bias attribute direction from the model's internal representations.

We use a total of five such prompts inherited from prior work \cite{howard2024uncovering and produce three different LMM responses to each image and prompt by varying the random seed.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Our main goal with evaluating our models is to directly measure the effects on bias in an open ended setting.
elements.sent-2411-076: In order to evaluate how open-ended LMM generations vary in response to images depicting people of different social attributes, we utilize images from three datasets which possess social attribute annotations: DA-COCO~\citep{chen2015microsoft,zhao2021captionbias , SocialCounterfactuals~\cite{howard2024socialcounterfactuals , and FACET~\cite{gustafson2023facet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The SocialCounterfactuals dataset contains 171k synthetic images depicting people of various races, genders, and physical characteristics such as age and body type.
elements.sent-2411-078: Images are grouped into counterfactual sets, in which each image is highly similar in its depiction of a common subject (e.g., a doctor) while varying only according to intersectional social attributes (e.g., race \& gender).
elements.sent-2411-079: We use separate subsets of the SocialCounterfactuals dataset for constructing ablation directions and evaluating models for bias related to race, gender, age, and body type.
elements.sent-2411-080: The Demographic Annotations on COCO (DA-COCO) dataset contains 28k images of people with annotations for skin tone and gender, which we use for evaluating bias related to perceived race and gender.
elements.sent-2411-081: Finally, the FACET dataset contains 32,000 diverse, high-resolution images annotated by experts, covering 13 person attributes and 52 person classes.
elements.sent-2411-082: For our evaluation, we utilize the perceived age, race, and gender attribute labels.
elements.sent-2411-083: An essential component of our method is that we can compute steering directions for LMMs from fully synthetic data, that can be successfully applied to real data.
elements.sent-2411-084: For each target attribute e.g. perceived race, age, physical appearance, and gender, we use synthetically generated images from two sources to compute steering directions: the SocialCounterfactuals dataset, and images generated with the Flux-1.0-dev image generator \cite{flux2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: In the case of SocialCounterfactuals, the images are generated with Stable Diffusion \citep{Rombach_2022_CVPR , and are constrained to be counterfactual with respect to the target attribute in question e.g. perceived race.}
elements.sent-2411-086: For Flux, images align with the target protected attributes but are not constrained to be counterfactuals.
elements.sent-2411-087: We measure the frequency at which LMMs mention an individual's protected attributes during open-ended generation, which can be undesirable in circumstances in which commenting on a person's race, age, gender, or body type would be considered offensive or inappropriate.
elements.sent-2411-088: While our evaluation method can be applied to any social attribute that relates to a set of target tokens, our evaluations focus on four protected attributes of perceived race, age, gender, body type.
elements.sent-2411-089: Due to the variety of ways in which such attributes can be referenced in natural language, we employ two alternative approaches for measuring their frequency: bigram counts and LLM-as-a-judge.
elements.sent-2411-090: We define a list of target words related to the attribute in question and detect all bigrams in model generations beginning with these words.
elements.sent-2411-091: Since many attribute-related terms are polysemous, we hand-annotate the most frequent bigrams to filter out unrelated terms.
elements.sent-2411-092: This enables us to adjust for over- or under-counting by including only those bigrams that have been verified as attribute-related or excluding those that have been annotated as unrelated.
elements.sent-2411-093: Despite being transparent and interpretable, this approach is limited in the sense that it may not capture all possible ways in which protected attributes could be referenced.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Loss Function Analysis for One Step Gradient-Based Optimization
elements.sent-2411-134: We investigated various loss functions to determine their suitability for gradient-based token manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-135: While many intuitive loss functions appeared promising, we found that they often yielded ineffective results.
elements.sent-2411-136: For instance, using the sigmoid activation on the logits led to nearly zero gradients in practice, as the logit values in the LLM are often high-magnitude, saturating the sigmoid function and nullifying gradients.
elements.sent-2411-137: To better understand the behavior of different loss functions in the context of our proposed feature optimization, we analyze the gradients of three key functions: KL Divergence, repeated Cross-Entropy, and Mean Squared Error.
elements.sent-2411-138: Each loss function is evaluated in terms of its impact on the gradient direction and magnitude with respect to the input logits of the LLM, denoted as \(\mathbf{z \in \mathbb{R ^V\) for a vocabulary size \(V\).}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Let \(\mathbf{p = \text{softmax (\mathbf{z )\) represent the probability vector obtained from the LLM logits, and let \(\mathcal{T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_N\}\) be the set of target tokens we aim to manipulate.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: We analyze the gradients of the following loss functions: KL-Divergence (KL), a column-wise Cross-Entropy (CE), and Mean Squared Error (MSE), to understand their impact on the feature optimization.
elements.sent-2411-141: Consequently, KL-Divergence provides weak gradients in settings where uniform probability across the target tokens is desired, limiting its effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-142: To shift the probability distribution towards individual tokens in \(\mathcal{T}\), we apply a repeated Cross-Entropy (CE) loss for each \(t \in \mathcal{T}\): \begin{equation}CE(p, T) = -\sum_{t \in T} \log(p_t)\end{equation} For a single token \(t\), the Cross-Entropy gradient with respect to \(\mathbf{z}\) is: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} (-\log(p_t)) = -( \delta_{tj} - p_j)\end{equation} where \(\delta_{tj}\) is 1 if \(j = t\) and 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-143: Summing over all tokens in \(\mathcal{T}\) yields: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} CE(p, T) = -\sum_{t \in T} (\delta_{tj} - p_j)\end{equation} This form introduces a ``tug-of-war'' effect, where each token \(t \in \mathcal{T}\) pulls the logits towards itself, creating interference among the tokens in \(\mathcal{T}\).
elements.sent-2411-144: The resulting gradient direction is not aligned with maximizing probabilities across all tokens in \(\mathcal{T}\) simultaneously, leading to inconsistent results.
elements.paragraph-2411-011: Mean Squared Error Loss with Target Logits
elements.sent-2411-145: Lastly, we minimize the MSE on the target tokens, setting a target logit value \(M\) for each token in \(\mathcal{T}\): \begin{equation}MSE(z, T, M) = \sum_{i \in T} (z_i - M)^2\end{equation} The gradient of this loss with respect to each logit \(z_j\) is: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} MSE(z, T, M) =  2(z_j - M) j \in T 0 j \notin T \end{equation} This gradient is directly proportional to \(z_j - M\), ensuring a strong directional push for each \(z_j\) in \(\mathcal{T}\) towards \(M\).
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} MSE(z, T, M) =  2(z_j - M) j \in T 0 j \notin T \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-146: Additionally, only logits corresponding to tokens in \(\mathcal{T}\) are affected, avoiding interference and aligning the gradient direction to effectively manipulate each token's logit towards the target value \(M\).
elements.sent-2411-147: Therefore, we run our Gradient-based Steering with MSE loss.
elements.sent-2411-148: Our findings underscore the substantial benefits of a rapid, training-free technique for model steering in large multi-modal models.
elements.sent-2411-149: By using a single gradient step, we effectively guide the model to downplay specific protected attributes, such as perceived age, race, or gender, in its outputs.
elements.sent-2411-150: Given the rapid deployment of LMMs across high-impact sectors, our approach represents a scalable and impactful method for aligning models with ethical and societal expectations without extensive retraining or re-tuning requirements.
elements.sent-2411-151: Despite our best efforts to improve the fairness of generative AI models, we acknowledge that our choice of models, methodologies, and datasets may themselves contain latent biases which limit our ability to address this multi-faceted problem.
elements.sent-2411-152: The advantages of this model-steering method are twofold: it is not only effective, but also incredibly efficient.
elements.sent-2411-153: Our approach leverages a single gradient step to reduce or remove attribute-based patterns in model outputs.
elements.sent-2411-154: By circumventing traditional training cycles, which can be prohibitively costly and time-consuming, this method achieves a streamlined pathway to targeted model adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-155: The elimination of a need for full model re-training or fine-tuning directly translates to significant savings in both energy and hardware resources.
elements.sent-2411-156: As AI practitioners grapple with the environmental impact of increasingly large models, our method contributes a sustainable alternative.

elements.sent-2411-208: Large Multi-Modal Models (LMMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities as general-purpose chatbots able to engage in conversations about visual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-209: In this work, we propose a training-free debiasing framework for LMMs that intervenes on the model's representations during text generation by constructing a steering vector that reduces reference on protected attributes.
elements.sent-2411-210: Our framework introduces two complementary methods: (1) a dataset-based approach that constructs a steering vector by contrasting model activations on biased and neutral inputs, and (2) a novel optimization-based approach designed for low-resource settings, which constructs the steering vector using a single step of gradient-based perturbation without requiring additional data.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our experiments show that these interventions effectively reduce the propensity of LMMs to generate text related to protected attributes while maintaining sentiment and fluency.
elements.sent-2411-212: Furthermore, we demonstrate that debiased LMMs achieve comparable accuracy to their unmodified counterparts on downstream tasks, indicating that bias mitigation can be achieved without sacrificing model performance.
elements.sent-2411-213: While this effect can be observed to some extent for most models, it is especially prevalent in models trained on web-scale data.
elements.sent-2411-214: Numerous prior works have observed and identified such biases present in modern Large Language Models (LLMs) \citep{bender2021dangers, bommasani2021opportunities , while other recent work shows that such biases are even more prevalent in Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) \footnote{LMMs refer specifically to generative multimodal models.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Without additional safety tuning, these pre-existing biases may be amplified further when an LLM is augmented with pretrained visual capabilities \citep{howard2024uncovering , which also come with a distinct set of implicit societal biases present in the visual pretraining data.}
elements.sent-2411-216: In this work, we address a form of such biases by seeking to reduce the propensity of an LMM to generate protected attribute-related text unnecessarily.
elements.sent-2411-217: While it is often benign and even useful to make use of protected attributes in generation, LMMs have been found to suffer from group-dependent sentiment disparities \citep{raj2024biasdora as well as conditional inaccuracies given certain protected attributes \citep{howard2024socialcounterfactuals, zhao2021captionbias .}}
elements.sent-2411-218: In many real-world applications, such group-conditional performance differences are undesirable.
elements.sent-2411-219: Therefore, our goal is to provide methods for mitigating this behavior via modification of the internal representations of an LMM with respect to a set of target protected attributes.

Middle: our PAR approach constructs a steering vector from a dataset of contrasting activations given by attribute-related Q\&A. Right: LMMs with basic debiasing mechanisms like reprompting are not effective for debiasing on real world data.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-220: A variety of methods have been proposed recently for debiasing LLMs and discriminative VLMs individually \citep{lin2024towards,slyman2024fairdedup .}
elements.sent-2411-221: In contrast, relatively little prior work has focused specifically on debiasing LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-222: One naive approach could be to simply define a list of words or symbols that are prohibited from being generated at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-223: However, a more nuanced approach is required for attributes with many or ambiguous meanings, such as perceived race (e.g., black, white)\footnote{Wherever we use words such as race or gender throughout this work, we mean perceived race and perceived gender.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Despite prior efforts \citep{howard2024uncovering , there remains no canonical recipe for constructing a debiasing dataset large enough to meet data requirements for training LMMs while also containing fine-grained social attribute annotations.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Our work addresses these limitations by introducing a training-free approach for debiasing LMMs which can be applied to any attribute at inference time without degrading model performance.
elements.sent-2411-226: Specifically, we propose a novel method for identifying and ablating (removing) biased representations within LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-227: Recent work has shown that LLMs tend to encode high-level concepts as linear directions in feature space \citep{templeton2024scaling , making it possible to steer a specific feature’s contribution to the model’s output.}
elements.sent-2411-228: For example, adding a “steering vector” can amplify a desired feature \citep{zou2023representation , whereas ablating that direction can remove the feature \citep{arditi2024refusal .}}
elements.sent-2411-229: We introduce two methods for constructing steering vectors: (1) a dataset-based approach, where steering vectors are computed offline by comparing model activations from contrasting datasets—one that provokes the feature of interest and one that does not, and (2) a novel optimization-based approach, which constructs the steering vector at inference time using just the input image and the target attribute for removal.
elements.sent-2411-230: Our methods differ from prior LLM steering work in two fundamental ways.
elements.sent-2411-231: First, our dataset-based method conditions the LMM on biased multimodal inputs to induce the target representations.
elements.sent-2411-232: Second, when no data is available, our optimization-based approach computes a steering vector at inference time using just the input image and the target attribute for removal.
elements.sent-2411-233: Instead of crafting a dataset of contrasting prompts, we only need to leverage the information already available to the LMM, the input image.
elements.sent-2411-234: Specifically, during inference we perform a single step of gradient descent on the visual representation in the direction of the target attribute(s).
elements.sent-2411-235: For both approaches, steering is performed by extracting downstream activations from a single layer that follow from both the perturbed and the unchanged visual representations.
elements.sent-2411-236: We propose an two training-free algorithms to find effective steering directions for LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-237: PAR (Protected Attribute Removal), that uses a dataset of biased inputs, and a novel method PARzero designed for low-resource settings.
elements.sent-2411-238: PARzero uses no extra data, and steers by performing a single optimization step on the input visual representation.
elements.sent-2411-239: We show that our proposed approaches can effectively debias two popular LMM model families LLaVA and Llama3.
elements.sent-2411-240: We analyze the steered and unsteered generations across all datasets and find that steering produces equitable group-level sentiment without degrading accuracy or language modeling capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-242: Prior work on controlling LLM outputs has explored interventions on model inputs, outputs, or intermediate representations.
elements.sent-2411-243: Intervening on intermediate model representations is the most related approach to our work.

elements.sent-2411-245: These features were found to be monosemantic -- able to locally augment downstream outputs when manually introduced during inference.

elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=.95\textwidth]{figs/architecture_diagram_v3.pdf} \caption{PARzero's gradient-based steering direction computation. (Step 1) Given an input image, prompt the LMM to produce text related to the target attribute and store the intermediate activations. (Step 2) Compute modified image tokens $\mathbf{u'}$ by performing a single optimization on the image tokens to maximize the downstream logits $\mathbf{z}$ corresponding to the target token indices, where $\bar{\mathbf{z}}$ is a copy of $\mathbf{z}$ with amplified target tokens. elements.sent-2411-317: Our findings underscore the substantial benefits of an efficient, training-free technique for model steering in large multi-modal models.
elements.sent-2411-318: We showed that PAR and PARzero can effectively guide the model to remove specific protected attribute text, such as perceived age, race, or gender, from its outputs.
elements.sent-2411-319: Given the rapid deployment of LMMs across high-impact sectors, our approach represents a scalable and impactful method for aligning models with ethical and societal expectations without extensive retraining or modification.
elements.sent-2411-320: Finally, our single-gradient-step approach PARzero offers a promising framework for further development in bias mitigation and model alignment.
elements.sent-2411-321: We also see an opportunity to explore techniques for steering different LMM architectures, given the different performance of LLaVA vs Llama.
